votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the Tribonacci recurrence and the specification checks the length and elements of the list exactly as the Coq code does.
The Python code correctly implements the Tribonacci sequence logic and the specification checks exactly match the Coq definitions.
The Python code correctly implements the Tribonacci sequence logic and the specification matches the Coq definition exactly.

The Python code faithfully translates the Coq code. The tri function matches: base cases (0->1, 1->3), even case returns 1 + n//2, odd case returns t_prev + tri(n-2) + t_next where t_prev = 1 + (n-1)//2 and t_next = 1 + (n+1)//2. The spec checks length is n+1 and each element at index i equals tri(i). Both use integer division which behaves the same for non-negative integers.

The Python code faithfully translates the Coq code. The tri function matches: base cases n=0 returns 1, n=1 returns 3; for n>=2, if even returns 1+n//2, if odd returns t_prev + tri(n-2) + t_next where t_prev=1+(n-1)//2 and t_next=1+(n+1)//2. The Coq pattern 'S (S k)' means n>=2 and k=n-2, so 'tri k' is 'tri(n-2)'. The spec checks length and all elements match tri(i). Integer division semantics match for non-negative integers.

The Python code faithfully translates the Coq code. The tri function matches: base cases n=0 returns 1, n=1 returns 3; for n>=2, if even returns 1+n//2, if odd returns t_prev + tri(n-2) + t_next where t_prev=1+(n-1)//2 and t_next=1+(n+1)//2. The Coq pattern 'S (S k)' means n>=2 and k=n-2, so 'tri k' is 'tri(n-2)'. The spec checks length and that each element equals tri(i), matching the Coq spec. Integer division in both uses floor division for naturals.
tri matches Coq for n>=0 (same base cases and even/odd branches with integer division), and the spec checks length n+1 and l[i]=tri(i) for 0<=i<=n, aligning with Coq.

Python tri matches Coq tri on all n >= 0: same base cases, even case uses 1 + n//2, odd case sums tri(n-2) and the even-formulas for n-1 and n+1. The spec checks length n+1 and l[i] = tri(i) for 0..n, matching Coq. Domain difference for negative n is irrelevant since Coq uses nat.
Python tri matches the Coq tri for all n ≥ 0 (same base cases, even/odd cases, and integer division), and the spec checks length n+1 and l[i]=tri(i) for i=0..n identically.
